sections.heading: The Art of Storytelling
sections.body:
Stories are 22x more memorable than facts alone. Every great presentation includes stories.

Story structure (4 elements):
1. Character: Someone the audience can relate to
2. Conflict: A challenge or problem they face
3. Resolution: How the challenge was overcome
4. Lesson: The takeaway for the audience

Where to use stories:
• Opening: A story that illustrates your main point
• Body: Examples that make abstract concepts concrete
• Closing: A story that inspires action

Story tips:
• Use specific details (not 'someone' — 'my colleague Priya')
• Show emotions (not 'she was nervous' — 'her hands were shaking')
• Keep it relevant (every story should serve a point)
• Practice the timing (stories can run long)

sections.heading: Vocal Variety and Delivery
sections.body:
Monotone kills presentations. Vary your voice:

Pace:
• Normal: 130-150 words per minute
• Slow down for emphasis
• Speed up for excitement
• Pause after key points (2-3 seconds)

Volume:
• Normal volume for most of the talk
• Increase for emphasis
• Decrease to draw people in (whisper technique)

Pitch:
• Higher pitch = excitement, questions
• Lower pitch = authority, seriousness
• Avoid uptalk (ending statements with rising pitch)

Pauses are powerful:
• Before a key point: 'And the winner is... [pause]... you.'
• After a key point: let it sink in
• To replace filler words (um, uh, like)

sections.heading: Handling Q&A Sessions
sections.body:
Before Q&A:
• Prepare 3-5 anticipated questions and answers
• Have backup slides with supporting data
• End with 'I'll take questions' not 'Any questions?'

During Q&A:
• Repeat the question (so everyone hears it)
• Answer concisely (30-60 seconds max)
• If you don't know: 'Great question — I'll follow up after the session'
• Bridge back to your key points: 'That connects to what I said about...'

Difficult situations:
• Hostile question: 'I appreciate the question. Here's my perspective...'
• Off-topic: 'Interesting point. Let's discuss that offline.'
• No questions: 'Let me ask YOU a question...' (engage the audience)
• Time's up: 'One last question...'

sections.heading: Presentation Aids
sections.body:
Slides should support, not replace, you:

The 10-20-30 Rule (Guy Kawasaki):
• 10 slides maximum
• 20 minutes maximum
• 30-point font minimum

Slide design:
• One idea per slide
• Use images over text
• Maximum 6 words per line, 6 lines per slide
• High contrast (dark on light or light on dark)
• No animations (distracting)

Other aids:
• Whiteboard: Good for impromptu explanations
• Props: Make abstract concepts tangible
• Video: Use sparingly, test beforehand
• Handouts: Give AFTER the presentation, not before

The rule: If the audience is reading your slides, they're not listening to you.

summary: Public speaking is a performance skill. Use stories to make points memorable, vary your voice to maintain attention, and structure your talk with a clear beginning, middle, and end.
